segments.text: quick fixes in solo lint for visual
studio are an effortless way to keep
your code clean
but what is a quick fix
we'll take this example of a simple
typedef if we hover over the highlight
we see that the sony recommendation is
that using should be preferred
a nice modernizing rule
but do we really want to go through all
of our code changing these perfectly
working type defs
well maybe
but now immediately below the rules
summary we'll see this quick fix action
and clicking on that will rewrite the
code for us
let's take a look at another one
now we can also get to the quick fix
action with alt enter or control dot so
mouse free fixing
now in those cases the type deaths were
readable enough and the transformations
fairly straightforward
the problem with type defs is that they
get harder to read the more levels of
indirection you add
following a typedef like this one is a
tricky and error-prone process for most
of us
so being able to quick fix it into a
using declaration automatically is a big
relief now the using keyword started out
as a way to deal with namespaces
but no spaces themselves have evolved a
lot since then too
in c plus 17 we got nested namespace
definitions
so we can clean up heavily nested code
easily
and now that's just one click away
but then in c plus 20 we got nested
inline namespaces which make all of
their declarations visible in the
enclosing namespace as well
and solo lint for visual studio also
handles this which is just as well
because the syntax can look a bit odd if
you're not used to it
now we've had move semantics since c
plus 11
but it can still be tricky to keep track
of what you can move and how
here we can see that this stud move is
highlighted
because it's being passed as a
constraint argument so it's not an r
value
we can quick fix to remove that
similarly with this one the type itself
has no move constructor so the call to
stood move does nothing we should remove
it
or this one is const so again
not movable
now if you're working with code from
before surplus plus 11 you might have a
lot of tediously spelled out types
especially iterators
very often these are not adding any
information as we know what to expect
from a call like so you begin here
so it's nice to be able to convert that
to auto in one click
but notice that this one is left
unhighlighted that's because we're
assigning the return of plane begin to a
const iterator there's a type conversion
here so changing this to auto would
change the behavior
now that may be fine although of course
if we'd started with a plane iterator
we'd have been offered the same
conversion
but the simplest option is to change
this to sleep again like in our first
example
perhaps you've been using auto with
begin and end without thinking about
their constants
maybe it's time to revisit that habit
the final example we'll look at involves
exception hierarchies
we can catch exceptions by their dynamic
type of course
but if multiple catch blocks could catch
a particular object then only the first
match is selected
so if as we have here a base class is
mentioned before a derived class
then the derived branch is never matched
which sona didn't warns us about
but sonalint also offers not just one
but three quick fixes here as there's a
few reasonable ways to address this
we could move the base after the derived
or the derived before the base
both of which will actually change the
behavior
or you could just remove the derived
catch block which actually preserves
behavior
you probably wanted one of the first
ones
and that's it that's been a sampling of
some of the many quick fixes we've added
to sonalin and available here in the
plugin for visual studio
the plugin is only a click away in the
marketplace so why not try it out
yourself today
